main_summary.summary: Ruby Sales, interviewed April 25, 2011 by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program in collaboration with the National Museum of African American History and Culture, recounts her trajectory from Columbus, Georgia to SNCC organizer in Lowndes County, Alabama. The daughter of a Baptist Army chaplain and an outspoken mother, Sales attended Carver High School and entered Tuskegee Institute at sixteen in 1963. After Stokely Carmichael and Lowndes County residents visited campus and after Tuskegee students were trapped at the Montgomery Capitol following Selma's Bloody Sunday, Sales abandoned her beatnik ambitions and joined SNCC's Lowndes County voter-registration project. She describes the August 1965 Fort Deposit demonstration, the jail in Hayneville, and the shooting at the Cash Store where Tom Coleman killed Episcopal seminarian Jonathan Daniels (who pulled Sales backward, taking the shot meant for her) and wounded Father Richard Morrisroe. Sales testified at Coleman's trial. She traces the long aftermath through Manhattanville, Princeton graduate study in history, and her current work directing the SpiritHouse Project on counterculture education, language and memory, mass incarceration, and what she terms the Southern Freedom Movement.
key_themes: Jonathan Daniels killing and Coleman trial
Black Southern education and Carver High School
main_summary.historical_significance:
Sales is the surviving witness to the August 20, 1965 killing of Jonathan Daniels at the Cash Store in Hayneville, Alabama, and her testimony at Tom Coleman's trial is part of the documentary record of that case. As a SNCC organizer in Lowndes County during the period that produced both Tent City and the Lowndes County Freedom Organization (whose black panther symbol was later adopted by the Black Panther Party), she provides first-person accounts of the Fort Deposit demonstration, the Hayneville jail conditions, and the internal SNCC debates about black-and-white-together organizing in the wake of the Voting Rights Act.

The interview also contributes a sustained analytical voice on language, narrative, and counterculture education. Sales rejects "civil rights movement" in favor of "Southern Freedom Movement," argues for "enslavement" over "slavery," and frames the post-1968 dismantling of black Southern schools (citing David Cecelski) as a deliberate cultural war. Her reflections on growing up in Columbus, Georgia under Carver High School principal S.P. Charleston and home teacher Marian Armstrong document the network of Black Southern educators (Vanessa Siddle Walker) whose pedagogical legacy she is attempting to preserve through SpiritHouse.

chapters.title: Family, Father, and the Army Years
chapters.summary: Sales describes her father, son of a formerly enslaved woman, who joined the First Assisi Civilian Conservation Camp, became a Baptist minister and Army chaplain, and was radicalized by the Korean War. She introduces her mother as someone who refused to submit to oppression and recounts her grandmother's town where Sales relatives constituted thirty percent of the population.

chapters.title: Fort Benning and Childhood in Columbus
chapters.summary: Sales contrasts the integrated world of Fort Benning, ten miles from Columbus, Georgia (with integrated buses, movie theaters, restaurants, and hospital under Truman's military integration) with the segregated civil society outside the base. She describes childhood acts of resistance, trying on bathing suits in white-only stores, sitting at the front of buses, that preceded any conscious political awakening.
related_events: Truman desegregation of the armed forces

chapters.title: Carver High School and Southern Black Education
chapters.summary: Sales describes Mother Mary Mission, then Carver High School in the Carver Heights subdivision of Columbus, Georgia. Principal S.P. Charleston and home teacher Marian Armstrong cultivated academic excellence and connected students to what Sales calls 'the project of freedom.' She characterizes the school as a counterculture of education built inside segregation, and identifies herself as a cheerleader, newspaper editor, Honor Society member, and rebel.

chapters.title: Tuskegee Institute and Movement Entry
chapters.summary: Sales arrives at Tuskegee Institute in fall 1963 at sixteen, intending to be a beatnik reading Virginia Woolf and quoting Allen Ginsberg. Gwen Patton brings Montgomery-bus-boycott movement consciousness to campus; Professor Charles Gomillion's gerrymandering challenge is part of the local atmosphere. Stokely Carmichael and Lowndes County residents visit campus and galvanize Sales, who cuts her ponytails and grows out her natural hair.

chapters.title: Bloody Sunday Aftermath at the Montgomery Capitol
chapters.summary: Following Selma's Bloody Sunday on the Edmund Pettus Bridge, Tuskegee students protest at the Montgomery Capitol and are locked in a church, surrounded by mounted officers with billy clubs and denied food and water. Sales loses her Southern Baptist faith waiting for a chariot of deliverance that never comes. She witnesses a SNCC woman face down a billy club, calls it the moment her 'little Columbus Georgia mind is blown,' and commits to the movement.

chapters.title: Lowndes County and the Freedom House
chapters.summary: Sales describes living in the SNCC freedom house in Lowndes County, where men and women slept in the same room as comrades rather than as sexual partners. She recounts the first day registering voters with Stokely Carmichael and Mary Nell Mosley, when the sheriff drew a gun and threatened to blow Carmichael's brains out, and Carmichael's reply that 'hell will be an integrated hell tonight.'

chapters.title: Local Leaders: Hulett, Clara Maul, Mr. Jackson
chapters.summary: Sales profiles John Hulett, an uneducated Lowndes County resident whom she contrasts with the college-educated leaders of her hometown, noting his consistent reliability and his refusal to hold the microphone. She describes Clara Maul, whose grandmother's relationship with a wealthy white man and ownership of a bar gave Maul a measure of protection that she used to drive SNCC organizers across the county at one hundred miles per hour.

chapters.title: Fort Deposit Demonstration and Hayneville Jail
chapters.summary: After the August 6, 1965 Voting Rights Act signing, young people from Fort Deposit propose a demonstration. SNCC debates whether to include Jonathan Daniels and Father Richard Morrisroe; Stokely Carmichael argues that excluding them would segregate the movement, and that position prevails. The demonstrators face a mob with baseball bats and garbage pails, are arrested onto a garbage truck, and held in the Hayneville jail where women are threatened with rape by trustees.

chapters.title: The Cash Store Killing
chapters.summary: Released suddenly from jail, the group walks to the corner store. Tom Coleman stands in the doorway with a gun and tells Sales, who is in front, that he will blow her brains out. Jonathan Daniels pulls Sales backward and is shot dead; Father Morrisroe is shot next and left begging for water in the sun. Joyce Bailey, seventeen, escapes alongside Sales. SNCC, John Doar, and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson spend days locating the bodies.

chapters.title: Testifying at the Coleman Trial
chapters.summary: Returning home, Sales finds her mother confronted in the backyard by white men threatening to kill Ruby if she testified; her mother nonetheless does not tell her to refuse. Sales testifies against one of Alabama's prominent defense attorneys, who attempts to insinuate that Sales was a whore and Daniels her white client. Sales describes shutting down emotionally for decades afterward, never mentioning her movement work at Princeton, Manhattanville, or in subsequent peace and justice work.

chapters.title: Tent City and the Lowndes County Freedom Organization
chapters.summary: Sales describes Tent City, where sharecroppers evicted from their land for registering to vote lived in tents rather than retreat. She locates Tent City within what she calls the second indigenous Southern Freedom Movement, traces the origin of the black panther symbol to Lowndes County before the Black Panther Party, and connects the work to lessons drawn from the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party.

chapters.title: Southern Freedom Movement and Historical Narrative
chapters.summary: Sales argues for the term 'Southern Freedom Movement' over 'civil rights movement' because the latter obscures the violence of segregation, the routine rape of black girls, and the long arc of black struggle. She critiques historians who frame Martin Luther King's training at Crozer and Boston University while missing the Southern preaching tradition King used in the pulpit (Vanessa Siddle Walker, Ralph Ellison, Mordecai Wyatt Johnson).

chapters.title: Language: 'Enslavement' and 'SNCC'
chapters.summary: Sales recounts correcting the defense attorney at the Coleman trial who referred to the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee as 'SNCC', a familiar form she felt he had no standing to use. As a history major at Princeton, she pushes for 'enslavement' over 'slavery' and for naming white enslavers as the active subjects of the historical record, against a discipline that names only the oppressed.

chapters.title: Memory, Dismembering, and the Cultural Wars
chapters.summary: Sales develops her concept of 'dismembering', the deliberate erasure of black agency in the historical record, and links it to the post-1960s cultural wars that turned liberation theology into prosperity gospel, fired 38,000 black teachers across thirteen Southern states between 1950 and 1962, and built the prison industrial complex. She cites David Cecelski's work on the destruction of black schools.

chapters.title: SpiritHouse and Counterculture Education
chapters.summary: Sales describes the SpiritHouse Project, drawing on the Greek pneuma (breath) to define social justice as how we live in relation to each other. Two recent years have focused on the counterculture of black Southern education during segregation, interviewing surviving teachers about their pedagogical strategies and connecting public schooling to the survival of HBCUs threatened with absorption into struggling white institutions.

loc_video.caption: Ruby Nell Sales oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Atlanta, Georgia, 2011 April 25
